    Sprint 0 – Problem Framing, Infrastructure Setup & RAG Foundations

    📄

    Implementing basic observability foundations

    Lesson 5

    Overview of Observability for LLM based applications


    In traditional software, observability means collecting logs, metrics, and traces to get visibility into the system’s internal state. For LLM applications, observability includes monitoring and understanding the LLM’s behavior (inputs, outputs, and intermediate steps) to ensure reliability. LLMs are non-deterministic, often producing unexpected or variable results for the same input. This unpredictability makes debugging more challenging than with standard software. Observability provides visibility into these “black box” models by capturing their interactions and performance data.


    Effective observability is crucial for several reasons:


    • Reliability & Debugging: When an LLM app returns an incorrect or odd response (or hits an error), developers need to quickly pinpoint why. Observability practices (structured logs, detailed traces, etc.) allow root cause analysis by tracing each request across all components. For example, if a chat assistant gives a wrong answer, good observability can reveal whether the issue was a bad prompt, a retrieval failure, or an LLM hallucination.

    • Optimisation & Performance: Users expect fast, efficient LLM apps, but these models can be slow or resource-intensive. Observability lets you measure key metrics (like latency and throughput) to find performance bottlenecks. By monitoring request latency, token usage, and system resource metrics, you can identify where the app is lagging and optimise it. Observability also helps with cost optimisation, since it tracks usage (e.g. tokens) and can highlight inefficiencies in prompts or model selection.

      • Note: most of the modern LLM Observability platforms, unfortunately, do not support native tracking of system resource metrics out of the box.

    • Quality Assurance: Development data might not reveal all real world interaction patterns, real users might trigger edge cases. Observability helps monitor output quality in production, e.g. we might track how often the model produces unsatisfactory or hallucinated answers. By logging outputs and any user feedback, you can catch when the application’s responses drift away from expectations.


    In summary, observability in LLM applications is about collecting and analyzing data (logs, metrics, traces) from the behavior of your application so that you can understand, debug, and improve a system that is generally unpredictable.


    The Three Pillars of Observability


    Modern observability is often described by the pillars of Logs, Metrics, and Traces. When it comes to LLM-based systems, we use these as following:



    Logging


    Logging means recording events and data during the application’s run - inputs, outputs, and any errors. In LLM applications, it is recommended to use structured logging for prompts and responses. This involves tracking:


    • User Inputs and Prompts: Log the prompts sent to the LLM (including system). This is key for debugging when an output is wrong - you can backtrack to what prompt produced it. This is also where you can apply prompt versioning by tracking versions of the prompt templates that have been used.

    • LLM Outputs: Record the raw outputs from the LLM. If your app then parses or transforms the output, you may log both the raw text and the parsed result. This data is very useful for analysing failures.

    • Errors and Exceptions: Log any errors from the LLM API (e.g. rate limit errors, timeouts) as well as application-side errors (exceptions in your code). Consistent error logging helps track failure rates and diagnose whether an issue is due to your application logic or an external API failing.

    • Metadata: Include additional metadata with each log entry, e.g. a timestamp, a request or session ID, model name used, etc. This context helps when filtering logs (e.g., find all outputs from a certain model version) and when investigating issues across a timeline.


    Logging in an LLM application is more extensive than in some traditional applications. We log all requests and responses for analysis. These logs enable you to later search for specific interactions (e.g., find all instances where the model said a particular incorrect fact) and to audit behavior.



    Metrics


    Metrics are numeric measures that track the performance and usage of your LLM application over time. You should identify a set of key metrics to monitor. Some of important metrics for LLM applications:


    • Latency: How long each request takes to process. You may track the end-to-end latency (from user request to final answer) as well as breakdowns (latency of the LLM API call vs. other steps like retrieval). Monitoring latency helps you spot regressions.

    • Throughput & Request Rates: How many requests are being handled (requests per second or per minute). This helps ensure your system can scale and stays within predefined cost boundaries. Tracking this metric also helps catch any spikes in the traffic, e.g. patterns within the day.

    • Token Usage: Track the number of input tokens and output tokens per request. This helps with cost monitoring and optimization, e.g. if a prompt is extremely long or the model is generating unnecessarily long outputs, you’ll see that in the metrics. Monitoring tokens per request (and total tokens over time) prevents unexpected cost spikes.

    • Prompt Length: Related to token usage, track lengths of prompts (in tokens or characters). If prompt templates or retrieved contexts grow too large, they can slow down responses or hit model limits. Keeping an eye on prompt sizes ensures you stay within model context limits and can optimize prompt construction.

    • Error Rates: Monitor the rate of errors: e.g., the percentage of requests that result in an error or failure. This could be broken down by error type. A rising error rate indicates reliability issues. For instance, if 5% of requests are hitting timeouts, that’s important to know and investigate.

    • Resource Usage: Ideally you should also track system metrics: CPU/GPU utilization, memory usage, etc. In many cases, LLM apps call external APIs, so this may be less relevant unless you manage your own model servers or vector databases.

    • Eval Scores: These are production evals, not to be confused with development evals. Within the budget, you should be running LLM evals on production traffic to spot any uncaught edge cases. This data is also critical for continuous development of the system.

      

    Collecting metrics allows you to build dashboards and track trends over time. For example, you might have a dashboard showing average latency and throughput each hour, and another showing total token consumption and cost per day.



    Tracing


    Tracing involves tracking the execution flow of each request through the different components of your LLM application. A trace represents a single user interaction from start to finish. It is composed of one or more spans, where each span corresponds to a specific step or operation in your workflow. 


    Here is an example trace visualisation for a naive Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) workflow.




    The Trace represents the end-to-end data flow. Starting at the user query and ending when the user receives an answer.


    The flow is then split into 3 main Spans:


    A. Query rewrite and enhancement.


    With a single sub-span:


    1. Rewrite the original user query.


    B. Retrieval.


    With two sub-spans:


    2. Embed the query.

    3. Retrieve top k nearest elements to the embedded query from the vector database.


    C. Generation.


    Also with two sub-spans:


    4. Render the prompt template by populating it with the user query and retrieved context.

    5. Run the rendered prompt through the LLM and return the generated answer to the user.


    Tracing is crucial for understanding how a complex LLM application executes, especially when there are multiple steps like in a chain or agent. In the above example, by looking at the trace, you can see which step took the most time - maybe the vector search was 500ms, and the LLM call was 2 seconds - and you can pinpoint bottlenecks.



    Here are some key benefits of tracing in LLM apps:


    • End-to-End Visibility: A full trace shows the path of a request through the system. For each user query, you can see all sub-operations - data retrieval, each tool the LLM might call (in an agent scenario), the LLM response generation, and any post-processing. This is especially useful in agentic or multi-step systems where the sequence is not static. Traces let you “replay” what happened in a timeline form.

    • Latency Breakdown: Traces capture timing for each span. This makes it obvious where time is being spent. For instance, if users complain something is slow, a trace might reveal that 90% of the time is spent in the LLM call vs. other steps. Or if using multiple tools, you might discover one API call (like a search step) is the slowest part.

    • Debugging Logic & Errors: Traces often include the inputs and outputs of each span. This is very helpful for debugging. If an error occurs, you can locate the span where it happened. Similarly, if the final answer was wrong, you can inspect intermediate spans - maybe the retrieval span had missing or irrelevant context, leading to a bad answer.

    • Understanding Agent Decisions: For LLM agents that make dynamic decisions (like tools usage), traces let you follow the chain-of-thought. This step-by-step view is critical to debug why an agent took a certain action or got stuck in a loop.


    In summary, observing an LLM application means watching both the system’s performance (latency, throughput, errors, costs) and the model’s behavior (what it’s saying, how often it’s wrong, how it’s handling inputs). By keeping dashboards and alerts on these aspects, an AI engineer can quickly detect when something’s off and iteratively improve the application.
